ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for samosa as a fried pastry snack; dahi chaat assembly is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fried filled pastry snacks; the full chaat assembly with yogurt and chutneys is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for samosa dahi chaat or equivalent Indian chaat dish as of 2026.

main_ingredients: samosa (refined flour pastry shell, potato filling, spices)

serving.description: 1 plate (approximately 1–2 samosas with yogurt and chutneys)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Samosa Dahi Chaat is approximately 200 g, comprising one to two samosas topped with generous amounts of yogurt, tamarind chutney, mint chutney, sev, and fresh coriander.

nutrient_source.title: Samosa Dahi Chaat — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 with review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Samosa Dahi Chaat (cr_id G00010926). Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from ICMR-NIN deep-fried samosa data (~4.5 g/100g) as a conservative approximation; documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Samosa and Potato-filled Fried Pastry — analogous estimate based on deep-fried potato-filled pastry (GI ~65) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Samosa Dahi Chaat in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated from the closest analogous food: deep-fried potato-filled pastry (GI ~65, medium category) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The yogurt component (low GI ~35) and tamarind chutney partially moderate the overall GI, but the dominant refined-flour fried pastry and potato filling anchor the composite GI in the medium range. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per 100g (33.16g) / 100 = 21.6 per 100g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 388 mg/100g with insufficient potassium (166 mg/100g) and fibre (1.8 g/100g) offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one small samosa and request chutneys on the side, as sodium at 388 mg/100g is in the borderline range and the low potassium-to-sodium ratio does not provide adequate cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate (33.16 g/100g), low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g), medium-high GI (~65), deep-fried preparation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Samosa Dahi Chaat due to its refined-flour pastry shell, high-GI potato filling, and low fibre content, which together produce a high glycaemic load of approximately 21.6 per 100g and risk rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 17.47 g/100g, estimated saturated fat ~4.5 g/100g, trans fat ~0.1 g/100g from deep-frying, sodium 388 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Samosa Dahi Chaat due to its high total fat content, estimated saturated fat from deep-frying, the presence of trans fat (~0.1 g/100g), and borderline sodium, all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generating AGEs, trans fat ~0.1 g/100g, nightshade vegetables (potato, chili)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Samosa Dahi Chaat as deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), trans fat is present at ~0.1 g/100g, and the potato and chili filling contains nightshade vegetables that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Samosa Dahi Chaat is a popular Indian street-food dish widely served in Singapore's Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. It consists of one or two deep-fried triangular samosas — crispy pastry shells made from refined wheat flour filled with spiced mashed potato and peas — broken open and topped with generous amounts of chilled plain yogurt (dahi/curd), sweet-sour tamarind chutney, spicy mint-coriander chutney, crispy fried sev (thin chickpea-flour noodles), and fresh coriander leaves. The dish is finished with a dusting of chaat masala, a tangy spice blend. The flavour profile is a complex interplay of crunchy, creamy, sweet, sour, and spicy elements. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 310 kcal, 5.14g protein, 17.47g fat, and 33.16g carbohydrate with only 1.8g dietary fibre, making it a calorie-dense, high-glycaemic dish. Sodium is 388 mg/100g. The deep-fried preparation contributes saturated and trans fats. It is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. It is vegetarian and halal-friendly. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a starter, snack, or light meal at lunch or dinner.
common_user_queries: Is samosa chaat high in sodium?
What is dahi chaat made of?

ingredient_synonyms.sev: fried chickpea noodles, besan sev, crispy noodles
ingredient_synonyms.chaat masala: tangy spice blend, Indian chaat spice mix
ingredient_synonyms.mint chutney: pudina chutney, green chutney, coriander-mint sauce
ingredient_synonyms.samosa: fried pastry, triangular fried snack, aloo samosa

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010926) with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. Saturated fat estimated from ICMR-NIN deep-fried samosa data as HPB does not report this field for this entry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for deep-fried potato-filled pastry.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.5 g/100g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN data for deep-fried samosa (Indian Food Composition Tables, NIN 2017) as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat for this entry. This is a conservative approximation and should be updated when HPB publishes a full fatty acid profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient vitamins, and moisture data for this assembled chaat dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN tables as of 2026. 3. trans_fat_g is set to 0.1 g/100g based on the input record's health_suitability assessment noting trans fat presence from deep-frying; this is an estimate consistent with ICMR-NIN data for deep-fried refined-flour pastry. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for deep-fried potato-filled pastry; no direct GI measurement for Samosa Dahi Chaat exists in the database as of 2026. 5. food_group corrected from 'Grains and staples' to 'Snacks' and food_subgroup from 'Baking ingredients' to 'Fried snacks and chaat'. 6. All core macros (protein_g 5.14, fat_g 17.47, carbohydrates_g 33.16) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
